mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Sweden on?
mapQuestions.answer: Europe — northern Europe, part of Scandinavia. 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Sweden?
mapQuestions.answer: Stockholm — a city built across 14 islands.

mapQuestions.question: Which countries border Sweden?
mapQuestions.answer: Norway to the west, Finland to the north-east.

mapQuestions.question: How much of Sweden is covered in forest?
mapQuestions.answer: About 70%. Sweden has more forest than any other European country.

mapQuestions.question: Why do parts of Sweden have 24 hours of daylight in summer?
mapQuestions.answer: The north of Sweden is inside the Arctic Circle. The sun doesn't set for weeks in summer, and barely rises in winter.

foods.name: Köttbullar
foods.description: Swedish meatballs in cream gravy with lingonberry jam and mashed potato. The IKEA cafeteria classic.

foods.name: Gravlax
foods.description: Salmon cured with salt, sugar, and dill — no cooking, just time. Served thin with mustard sauce on dark bread.

foods.name: Knäckebröd
foods.description: Crispy rye crackers — Swedish bread you can store for months. Top with butter and cheese.

foods.name: Fika
foods.description: Not really a food — Fika is the Swedish habit of stopping work for coffee and a cinnamon bun (kanelbulle) twice a day. Sacred.

foods.name: Lingonberry jam
foods.description: Tart red berries that grow wild in the forests. Swedes put the jam on meat as well as toast.

foods.name: Surströmming
foods.description: Fermented herring, opened outdoors only. Famous for being the world's smelliest food. (Optional!)

scavengerItems.label: Loads of bicycles locked up outside a building
A forest reaching right up to a town
A reindeer or moose road-warning sign
People doing fika in a café (look at the cinnamon buns)
A swimming spot in a lake or sea with steps and a ladder
A Swedish flag flying from a private garden
Birch trees with bright white papery bark
A traditional red wooden house with white trim
A sauna (in a garden, a hotel, or by a lake)

geoMatches.description: The capital, built across 14 islands joined by 50+ bridges. Old town (Gamla Stan), grand royal palace, and crystal-clear water you can swim in from the city.
Sweden's second city on the west coast. Big port, canals, fish markets, and Liseberg amusement park.
Far north Sweden, inside the Arctic Circle. Northern Lights in winter, midnight sun in summer, home of the Sámi people and reindeer herds.
A big island in the Baltic Sea with medieval walls, sheep farms, and warmer weather than the mainland.
A tiny mountain station in the north — one of the best places in the world to see the Aurora Borealis.

senses.smell: Pine forest, cinnamon buns, smoked salmon, woodsmoke from saunas, cold clean air…
senses.hear: Bicycle bells, quiet trains, lake water lapping, "Hej!" from strangers, distant church bells…
senses.taste: Salty cured fish, sweet cinnamon, tart lingonberry, strong coffee, cold spring water…
senses.feel: Cold lake water, hot sauna steam, soft moss, smooth birch bark, crunchy snow underfoot…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: What is "Fika" and why is it such a big deal?
stories.body: Fika (FEE-kah) is the Swedish habit of stopping work twice a day — usually around 10am and 3pm — for coffee, a cinnamon bun, and a chat. It's so important that big Swedish companies often have official Fika breaks built into the working day. Universities have Fika rooms. Friends Fika together at weekends. It's about slowing down, paying attention to each other, and not rushing. Even busy people make time for Fika. Some say it's the secret to Swedish happiness.
stories.thinkingQuestion: What's your version of Fika — a small habit that makes you happier when you stop and do it?

stories.question: How does Sweden produce so much music we all know?
stories.body: Sweden has 10 million people but produces an astonishing amount of the world's pop music. ABBA (1970s), Roxette (1980s-90s), Avicii, Robyn, and many of today's biggest hits are written by Swedish songwriters. Max Martin (a Swede) has written more #1 hits than anyone except John Lennon and Paul McCartney — songs for Britney Spears, Backstreet Boys, Taylor Swift, The Weeknd. One reason: every Swedish child gets free music lessons through the school system. Music isn't a luxury, it's a normal part of growing up.

stories.question: Where did IKEA come from?
stories.body: In 1943, a 17-year-old farm boy named Ingvar Kamprad started a tiny mail-order company in the small village of Älmhult, southern Sweden. He sold pens, picture frames, watches. He named it after his initials (IK), his farm (Elmtaryd), and his village (Agunnaryd) → IKEA. He started selling flat-pack furniture in 1956 because his designer's table wouldn't fit in a car. Today IKEA has 400+ stores in 50 countries, and about 10% of all babies in the world today were conceived in an IKEA bed. (That's a real statistic from IKEA itself.)

stories.question: Who are the Sámi people?
stories.body: The Sámi (also written Saami) are the indigenous people of the far north — they've lived in northern Sweden, Norway, Finland, and Russia for at least 5,000 years, long before any of those countries existed. They have their own languages, their own flag, their own parliament, and a traditional way of life based on reindeer herding. For centuries Nordic governments tried to force Sámi to give up their language and culture; only recently has that started to be undone. Today there are about 80,000 Sámi, and many still herd reindeer, though most have other jobs too.

stories.question: Why is Sweden so famous for being "neutral" and welcoming refugees?
stories.body: Sweden has avoided wars for over 200 years (since 1814), like Switzerland. After WWII Sweden became known for its strong social safety net — free healthcare, free education through university, generous parental leave (480 days per child!), unemployment support. This wealth and stability meant Sweden has taken in many refugees over the decades, especially from conflicts in former Yugoslavia, Iraq, and Syria. About 20% of people in Sweden today were born in another country — high for Europe. Sweden joined NATO in 2024, ending centuries of neutrality, because of the war in Ukraine.
stories.thinkingQuestion: A country that gives a lot of public benefits has to charge high taxes (Sweden's are around 50%). Is that a fair trade? Why or why not?

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you've eaten in Sweden so far?
Could you get used to Fika twice a day for the rest of your life?
In summer the sun barely sets in northern Sweden. Sleep in light or dark — which would you find harder?
Swedes don't do small talk and aren't loud in public. Refreshing or weird?
What's one Swedish habit you'd like to bring home with you?
Sweden ranks in the top 5 happiest countries in the world. What do you think makes a country happy?

wordSearch.hint: Swedish foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Moose (älg)
animals.description: Sweden's "king of the forest". Around 350,000 live in the woods. Best spotted at dusk.

animals.name: Red squirrel
animals.description: Russet with tufted ears, in any forest or city park.

animals.name: Reindeer
animals.description: Herded by the Sámi in the north. Can wander onto roads, especially around Kiruna.

animals.name: European beaver
animals.description: Common in Swedish rivers and lakes. Look for gnawed tree stumps.

animals.name: Common crane
animals.description: Spring and autumn — thousands migrate through Lake Hornborga in a famous "crane dance" gathering.

animals.name: Brown bear
animals.description: About 3,000 in Sweden's north and central forests. Shy — they almost always hear you first.

animals.name: Wolf
animals.description: Around 400 wolves in Sweden, mostly in the south-central forests. Strictly protected.
